Wikidata name: Sakanoue no Tamuramaro [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese general and shogun (758 – 811)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2020}} {{family name hatnote|Sakanoue|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro sw.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai}}]] {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Tamuramaro'''|坂上 田村麻呂||758{{spnd}}June 17, 811}} was a {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=court noble}}, general and ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' of the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. He served as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Minister of War}} and ''Ukon'e no Taisho'' (Major Captain of the Right Division of Inner Palace Guards). He held the ''[[kabane]]'' of [[Sukune|Ōsukune]] and the court Rank of [[Junior Second Rank]] and was awarded the {{ill|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|en|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|WD=Q859418|lt=Order of Second Class}}.
He was the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. He was considered an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar}} of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} in legend.<ref>Iwao, Seiichi. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&dq=Sakanoue+no+Karitamaro&pg=PA2329 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon'', p. 2329.]</ref>
==Military career== Serving [[Emperor Kanmu]], Tamuramaro was appointed ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' and given the task of conquering the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi}} (蝦夷征伐 ''Emishi Seibatsu''), a people native to the north of Honshū, which he subjugated. Recent evidence{{fact|date=February 2022}} suggests that a migration of Emishi from northern Honshū to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] took place sometime between the seventh and eighth centuries, perhaps as a direct result of this policy that pre-dated Tamuramaro's appointment. However, many Emishi remained in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region}} as subjects of the expanding {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese Empire and later established independent Fushu domains. After Emperor Kanmu's death, the general continued to serve {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei}} and {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga}} as {{nihongo|Major Counselor|大納言|''dainagon''}} and {{nihongo|[[:en:Hyōbu-shō|Minister of War]]|兵部卿|''Hyōbu-kyō''}}.<ref>Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p. 272.</ref> He was the second person to be given the title of ''Sei-i Taishōgun'' (征夷大将軍). The first to receive this title was {{ill|Ōtomo no Otomaro|en|Ōtomo no Otomaro|WD=Q2089860|lt=Ōtomo no Otomaro}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows]] It is said that the famous {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|WD=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata}} festivals and parades of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} (also celebrated in the city of Sendai in Miyagi prefecture), which draw over 3 million people to the prefecture a year, were popularised in remembrance of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's campaign to subdue the tribal societies then living in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]]. These annual ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' are called the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=''Nebuta'' festival in Aomori City}} and ''Neputa'' Festival ({{lang|ja|ねぷた祭り}}) in [[:en:Hirosaki City|Hirosaki City]]. They feature a number of gigantic, specially-constructed, illuminated paper floats. These huge festival structures are colourfully painted with mythical figures, and teams of men carry them through the streets as crowds shout encouragement. Aomori's great nebuta lanterns are said to hark back to Tamuramaro's innovative strategy in that early ninth century campaign. According to legend, the taishogun is remembered for having ordered huge illuminated lanterns to be placed at the top of hills; and when the curious Emishi approached these bright lights to investigate, they were captured and subdued.<ref>Boroff, Nicholas. ''National Geographic Traveler Japan'', p. 156.</ref> Until the mid-1990s the prize awarded for the best float of the parades was called the Tamuramaro Prize. However, there is no historical record that Tamuramaro went farther north than {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}}
Tamuramaro's name is linked with payments for construction projects at [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu Temple]] (''Kiyomizu-dera'') in the late 8th century.<ref>Kyoto University of Foreign Studies: [http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/7a154c954fe15a0f49257018002ebff5!OpenDocument Japan 101]</ref>
The {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} tells a story of when {{ill|Fujiwara no Toshihito|en|Fujiwara no Toshihito|WD=Q3090673|lt=Fujiwara no Toshihito}} had fought together with Sakanoue no Tamuramaro to subdue a gang of Emishi bandits that had stood in their way when the duo were passing through the Taya Caves on their way back to Kamakura.
[[File:Kuniyoshi Tsuchiyama.jpg|thumb|''The 53 stations of the Tōkaidō in pairs'' by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] depicting, from the left, the demon of Mount Suzuka, Suzuka Gozen and Sakanoue no Tamuramaro led by her to kill the demon Otakemaru.]]
==Death, memorial and legends== In the year 811 (''[[:en:Kōnin|Kōnin]] 2, 3rd month''), Tamuramaro died at age 54, to the great regret of Emperor Saga, who expressed his sense of loss by distributing large quantities of silk cloth, cotton cloth and rice in honour of his dead counsellor. His bow, arrows, quiver and sword were placed in his coffin by order of the Emperor.<ref name="Titsingh, p. 99">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', p. 99.]</ref>
The Tomb of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro is located in Kanshuji Higashikurisunocho, Yamashina Ward, Kyoto, Japan. He is not the samurai buried at Shōgun-zuka as that was a ceremonial statue of a warrior buried by Emperor Kanmu when he decided to move the capital to Heian-kyo, present day Kyoto.{{cn|date=June 2020}} His descendant {{ill|Urabe no Suetake|en|Urabe no Suetake|WD=Q7899535|lt=Urabe no Suetake}} inherited his bow.
After his death, Tamuramaro became a legendary figure featured in many stories. In these legends, Tamuramaro is not always a warrior who wages war against barbarians, but a hero who exterminates ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni}}'' and bandits in various areas. According to legend, {{ill|Ootakemaru|en|Ootakemaru|WD=Q30926182|lt=Ootakemaru}}, one of the Great Three Evil Yokai of Japan, was slain by Tamuramaro. Other stories connect him to {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|WD=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen}}, whom he marries and/or slays.{{cn|date=May 2024}} He had a daughter with her named Shourin. According to the Otogi-zoshi, Shourin is said to have become the guardian deity of the Suzuka Mountains, while in the Okujoruri, it is said she ascended to the level of the Jizō Bodhisattva after her death.
==Ancestry== According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', an official historical record, the Sakanoue clan is descended from {{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=Emperor Ling}} of the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty}}.<ref>[[Shoku Nihongi]] 延暦四年六月の条:右衛士督従三位兼下総守坂上大忌寸苅田麿ら表を上りて言さく、「臣らは、本是れ{{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=後漢霊帝}}の曾孫阿智王の後なり。漢の祚、魏に遷れるとき、阿智王、神牛の教に因りて、出でて帯方に行きて忽ち宝帯の瑞を得たり。</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collectionsonline.lacma.org/mwebcgi/mweb.exe?request=record;id=104465;type=101|title=Sakanoue no Karitamaro Drawing His Bow|publisher=[[Los Angeles County Museum of Art]]|accessdate=April 2, 2011}}</ref> The Sakanoue clan's {{ill|Family tree|en|Family tree|WD=Q189977|lt=Family tree}} shows that Tamuramaro is a 14th-generation descendant of Emperor Ling.<ref>塙 保己一 [Hanawa hokii] 続群書類従 [Zoku Gunsho Ruiju Series] 従十八巻坂上系図 [Volume 18 Sakagami Clan's family tree]</ref> Other research traces the origins of the Sakanoue clan from the Asian mainland, possibly through [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |title=Yosha Research |access-date=April 4, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120729043128/http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |archive-date=July 29, 2012 }}</ref>

Tamuramaro]][[zh:坂上田村麻呂]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro.jpg|birth_date=0758|death_date={{death date|0811|6|17}}|death_place={{ill|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142}}|title={{ill|Chamberlain of Japan|WD=Q2948088}}; {{ill|Chinjufu shōgun|WD=Q2700052}}; {{ill|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382}}; {{ill|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706}}; {{ill|Konoe Daisho|WD=Q11638972}}; {{ill|Mutsu no Azechi|WD=Q11658599}}; {{ill|Sangi|WD=Q1133663}}; {{ill|shogun|WD=Q131767}}|spouse={{ill|Miyoshi Takako|WD=Q100453655}}|children={{ill|Kiyono Sakanoue|WD=Q11424802}}; {{ill|Q106239190|WD=Q106239190}}; {{ill|Q110454274|WD=Q110454274}}; {{ill|Q110454899|WD=Q110454899}}; {{ill|Q11424777|WD=Q11424777}}; {{ill|Q11424783|WD=Q11424783}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Haruku|WD=Q11424791}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Hirono|WD=Q11424782}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Masano|WD=Q11424800}}|father={{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722}}|relatives={{ill|Q11424817|WD=Q11424817}}; {{ill|Q45828832|WD=Q45828832}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Matako|WD=Q48764642}}|family={{ill|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}}|native_name=坂上田村麻呂|resting_place={{ill|Nishinoyama Kofun|WD=Q11629758}}}} {{Short description|Japanese general and shogun (758 – 811)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2020}} {{family name hatnote|Sakanoue|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro sw.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai}}]] {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Tamuramaro'''|坂上 田村麻呂||758{{spnd}}June 17, 811}} was a {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=court noble}}, general and ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' of the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. He served as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Minister of War}} and ''Ukon'e no Taisho'' (Major Captain of the Right Division of Inner Palace Guards). He held the ''[[kabane]]'' of [[Sukune|Ōsukune]] and the court Rank of [[Junior Second Rank]] and was awarded the {{ill|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|en|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|WD=Q859418|lt=Order of Second Class}}.
He was the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. He was considered an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar}} of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} in legend.<ref>Iwao, Seiichi. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&dq=Sakanoue+no+Karitamaro&pg=PA2329 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon'', p. 2329.]</ref>
==Military career== Serving [[Emperor Kanmu]], Tamuramaro was appointed ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' and given the task of conquering the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi}} (蝦夷征伐 ''Emishi Seibatsu''), a people native to the north of Honshū, which he subjugated. Recent evidence{{fact|date=February 2022}} suggests that a migration of Emishi from northern Honshū to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] took place sometime between the seventh and eighth centuries, perhaps as a direct result of this policy that pre-dated Tamuramaro's appointment. However, many Emishi remained in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region}} as subjects of the expanding {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese Empire and later established independent Fushu domains. After Emperor Kanmu's death, the general continued to serve {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei}} and {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga}} as {{nihongo|Major Counselor|大納言|''dainagon''}} and {{nihongo|[[:en:Hyōbu-shō|Minister of War]]|兵部卿|''Hyōbu-kyō''}}.<ref>Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p. 272.</ref> He was the second person to be given the title of ''Sei-i Taishōgun'' (征夷大将軍). The first to receive this title was {{ill|Ōtomo no Otomaro|en|Ōtomo no Otomaro|WD=Q2089860|lt=Ōtomo no Otomaro}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows]] It is said that the famous {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|WD=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata}} festivals and parades of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} (also celebrated in the city of Sendai in Miyagi prefecture), which draw over 3 million people to the prefecture a year, were popularised in remembrance of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's campaign to subdue the tribal societies then living in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]]. These annual ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' are called the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=''Nebuta'' festival in Aomori City}} and ''Neputa'' Festival ({{lang|ja|ねぷた祭り}}) in [[:en:Hirosaki City|Hirosaki City]]. They feature a number of gigantic, specially-constructed, illuminated paper floats. These huge festival structures are colourfully painted with mythical figures, and teams of men carry them through the streets as crowds shout encouragement. Aomori's great nebuta lanterns are said to hark back to Tamuramaro's innovative strategy in that early ninth century campaign. According to legend, the taishogun is remembered for having ordered huge illuminated lanterns to be placed at the top of hills; and when the curious Emishi approached these bright lights to investigate, they were captured and subdued.<ref>Boroff, Nicholas. ''National Geographic Traveler Japan'', p. 156.</ref> Until the mid-1990s the prize awarded for the best float of the parades was called the Tamuramaro Prize. However, there is no historical record that Tamuramaro went farther north than {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}}
Tamuramaro's name is linked with payments for construction projects at [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu Temple]] (''Kiyomizu-dera'') in the late 8th century.<ref>Kyoto University of Foreign Studies: [http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/7a154c954fe15a0f49257018002ebff5!OpenDocument Japan 101]</ref>
The {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} tells a story of when {{ill|Fujiwara no Toshihito|en|Fujiwara no Toshihito|WD=Q3090673|lt=Fujiwara no Toshihito}} had fought together with Sakanoue no Tamuramaro to subdue a gang of Emishi bandits that had stood in their way when the duo were passing through the Taya Caves on their way back to Kamakura.
[[File:Kuniyoshi Tsuchiyama.jpg|thumb|''The 53 stations of the Tōkaidō in pairs'' by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] depicting, from the left, the demon of Mount Suzuka, Suzuka Gozen and Sakanoue no Tamuramaro led by her to kill the demon Otakemaru.]]
==Death, memorial and legends== In the year 811 (''[[:en:Kōnin|Kōnin]] 2, 3rd month''), Tamuramaro died at age 54, to the great regret of Emperor Saga, who expressed his sense of loss by distributing large quantities of silk cloth, cotton cloth and rice in honour of his dead counsellor. His bow, arrows, quiver and sword were placed in his coffin by order of the Emperor.<ref name="Titsingh, p. 99">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', p. 99.]</ref>
The Tomb of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro is located in Kanshuji Higashikurisunocho, Yamashina Ward, Kyoto, Japan. He is not the samurai buried at Shōgun-zuka as that was a ceremonial statue of a warrior buried by Emperor Kanmu when he decided to move the capital to Heian-kyo, present day Kyoto.{{cn|date=June 2020}} His descendant {{ill|Urabe no Suetake|en|Urabe no Suetake|WD=Q7899535|lt=Urabe no Suetake}} inherited his bow.
After his death, Tamuramaro became a legendary figure featured in many stories. In these legends, Tamuramaro is not always a warrior who wages war against barbarians, but a hero who exterminates ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni}}'' and bandits in various areas. According to legend, {{ill|Ootakemaru|en|Ootakemaru|WD=Q30926182|lt=Ootakemaru}}, one of the Great Three Evil Yokai of Japan, was slain by Tamuramaro. Other stories connect him to {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|WD=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen}}, whom he marries and/or slays.{{cn|date=May 2024}} He had a daughter with her named Shourin. According to the Otogi-zoshi, Shourin is said to have become the guardian deity of the Suzuka Mountains, while in the Okujoruri, it is said she ascended to the level of the Jizō Bodhisattva after her death.
==Ancestry== According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', an official historical record, the Sakanoue clan is descended from {{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=Emperor Ling}} of the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty}}.<ref>[[Shoku Nihongi]] 延暦四年六月の条:右衛士督従三位兼下総守坂上大忌寸苅田麿ら表を上りて言さく、「臣らは、本是れ{{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=後漢霊帝}}の曾孫阿智王の後なり。漢の祚、魏に遷れるとき、阿智王、神牛の教に因りて、出でて帯方に行きて忽ち宝帯の瑞を得たり。</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collectionsonline.lacma.org/mwebcgi/mweb.exe?request=record;id=104465;type=101|title=Sakanoue no Karitamaro Drawing His Bow|publisher=[[Los Angeles County Museum of Art]]|accessdate=April 2, 2011}}</ref> The Sakanoue clan's {{ill|Family tree|en|Family tree|WD=Q189977|lt=Family tree}} shows that Tamuramaro is a 14th-generation descendant of Emperor Ling.<ref>塙 保己一 [Hanawa hokii] 続群書類従 [Zoku Gunsho Ruiju Series] 従十八巻坂上系図 [Volume 18 Sakagami Clan's family tree]</ref> Other research traces the origins of the Sakanoue clan from the Asian mainland, possibly through [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |title=Yosha Research |access-date=April 4, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120729043128/http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |archive-date=July 29, 2012 }}</ref>

Tamuramaro]][[zh:坂上田村麻呂]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro.jpg|birth_date=0758|death_date={{death date|0811|6|17}}|death_place={{ill|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142}}|title={{ill|Chamberlain of Japan|WD=Q2948088}}; {{ill|Chinjufu shōgun|WD=Q2700052}}; {{ill|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382}}; {{ill|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706}}; {{ill|Konoe Daisho|WD=Q11638972}}; {{ill|Mutsu no Azechi|WD=Q11658599}}; {{ill|Sangi|WD=Q1133663}}; {{ill|shogun|WD=Q131767}}|spouse={{ill|Miyoshi Takako|WD=Q100453655}}|children={{ill|Kiyono Sakanoue|WD=Q11424802}}; {{ill|Q106239190|WD=Q106239190}}; {{ill|Q110454274|WD=Q110454274}}; {{ill|Q110454899|WD=Q110454899}}; {{ill|Q11424777|WD=Q11424777}}; {{ill|Q11424783|WD=Q11424783}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Haruku|WD=Q11424791}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Hirono|WD=Q11424782}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Masano|WD=Q11424800}}|father={{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722}}|relatives={{ill|Q11424817|WD=Q11424817}}; {{ill|Q45828832|WD=Q45828832}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Matako|WD=Q48764642}}|family={{ill|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477}}|native_name=坂上田村麻呂|resting_place={{ill|Nishinoyama Kofun|WD=Q11629758}}}} {{Short description|Japanese general and shogun (758 – 811)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2020}} {{family name hatnote|Sakanoue|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro sw.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai}}]] {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Tamuramaro'''|坂上 田村麻呂||758{{spnd}}June 17, 811}} was a {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=court noble}}, general and ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' of the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. He served as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Minister of War}} and ''Ukon'e no Taisho'' (Major Captain of the Right Division of Inner Palace Guards). He held the ''[[kabane]]'' of [[Sukune|Ōsukune]] and the court Rank of [[Junior Second Rank]] and was awarded the {{ill|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|en|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|WD=Q859418|lt=Order of Second Class}}.
He was the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. He was considered an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar}} of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} in legend.<ref>Iwao, Seiichi. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&dq=Sakanoue+no+Karitamaro&pg=PA2329 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon'', p. 2329.]</ref>
==Military career== Serving [[Emperor Kanmu]], Tamuramaro was appointed ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' and given the task of conquering the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi}} (蝦夷征伐 ''Emishi Seibatsu''), a people native to the north of Honshū, which he subjugated. Recent evidence{{fact|date=February 2022}} suggests that a migration of Emishi from northern Honshū to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] took place sometime between the seventh and eighth centuries, perhaps as a direct result of this policy that pre-dated Tamuramaro's appointment. However, many Emishi remained in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region}} as subjects of the expanding {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese Empire and later established independent Fushu domains. After Emperor Kanmu's death, the general continued to serve {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei}} and {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga}} as {{nihongo|Major Counselor|大納言|''dainagon''}} and {{nihongo|[[:en:Hyōbu-shō|Minister of War]]|兵部卿|''Hyōbu-kyō''}}.<ref>Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p. 272.</ref> He was the second person to be given the title of ''Sei-i Taishōgun'' (征夷大将軍). The first to receive this title was {{ill|Ōtomo no Otomaro|en|Ōtomo no Otomaro|WD=Q2089860|lt=Ōtomo no Otomaro}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows]] It is said that the famous {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|WD=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata}} festivals and parades of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}} (also celebrated in the city of Sendai in Miyagi prefecture), which draw over 3 million people to the prefecture a year, were popularised in remembrance of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's campaign to subdue the tribal societies then living in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]]. These annual ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' are called the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=''Nebuta'' festival in Aomori City}} and ''Neputa'' Festival ({{lang|ja|ねぷた祭り}}) in [[:en:Hirosaki City|Hirosaki City]]. They feature a number of gigantic, specially-constructed, illuminated paper floats. These huge festival structures are colourfully painted with mythical figures, and teams of men carry them through the streets as crowds shout encouragement. Aomori's great nebuta lanterns are said to hark back to Tamuramaro's innovative strategy in that early ninth century campaign. According to legend, the taishogun is remembered for having ordered huge illuminated lanterns to be placed at the top of hills; and when the curious Emishi approached these bright lights to investigate, they were captured and subdued.<ref>Boroff, Nicholas. ''National Geographic Traveler Japan'', p. 156.</ref> Until the mid-1990s the prize awarded for the best float of the parades was called the Tamuramaro Prize. However, there is no historical record that Tamuramaro went farther north than {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}}
Tamuramaro's name is linked with payments for construction projects at [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu Temple]] (''Kiyomizu-dera'') in the late 8th century.<ref>Kyoto University of Foreign Studies: [http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/7a154c954fe15a0f49257018002ebff5!OpenDocument Japan 101]</ref>
The {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} tells a story of when {{ill|Fujiwara no Toshihito|en|Fujiwara no Toshihito|WD=Q3090673|lt=Fujiwara no Toshihito}} had fought together with Sakanoue no Tamuramaro to subdue a gang of Emishi bandits that had stood in their way when the duo were passing through the Taya Caves on their way back to Kamakura.
[[File:Kuniyoshi Tsuchiyama.jpg|thumb|''The 53 stations of the Tōkaidō in pairs'' by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] depicting, from the left, the demon of Mount Suzuka, Suzuka Gozen and Sakanoue no Tamuramaro led by her to kill the demon Otakemaru.]]
==Death, memorial and legends== In the year 811 (''[[:en:Kōnin|Kōnin]] 2, 3rd month''), Tamuramaro died at age 54, to the great regret of Emperor Saga, who expressed his sense of loss by distributing large quantities of silk cloth, cotton cloth and rice in honour of his dead counsellor. His bow, arrows, quiver and sword were placed in his coffin by order of the Emperor.<ref name="Titsingh, p. 99">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', p. 99.]</ref>
The Tomb of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro is located in Kanshuji Higashikurisunocho, Yamashina Ward, Kyoto, Japan. He is not the samurai buried at Shōgun-zuka as that was a ceremonial statue of a warrior buried by Emperor Kanmu when he decided to move the capital to Heian-kyo, present day Kyoto.{{cn|date=June 2020}} His descendant {{ill|Urabe no Suetake|en|Urabe no Suetake|WD=Q7899535|lt=Urabe no Suetake}} inherited his bow.
After his death, Tamuramaro became a legendary figure featured in many stories. In these legends, Tamuramaro is not always a warrior who wages war against barbarians, but a hero who exterminates ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni}}'' and bandits in various areas. According to legend, {{ill|Ootakemaru|en|Ootakemaru|WD=Q30926182|lt=Ootakemaru}}, one of the Great Three Evil Yokai of Japan, was slain by Tamuramaro. Other stories connect him to {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|WD=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen}}, whom he marries and/or slays.{{cn|date=May 2024}} He had a daughter with her named Shourin. According to the Otogi-zoshi, Shourin is said to have become the guardian deity of the Suzuka Mountains, while in the Okujoruri, it is said she ascended to the level of the Jizō Bodhisattva after her death.
==Ancestry== According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', an official historical record, the Sakanoue clan is descended from {{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=Emperor Ling}} of the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty}}.<ref>[[Shoku Nihongi]] 延暦四年六月の条:右衛士督従三位兼下総守坂上大忌寸苅田麿ら表を上りて言さく、「臣らは、本是れ{{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=後漢霊帝}}の曾孫阿智王の後なり。漢の祚、魏に遷れるとき、阿智王、神牛の教に因りて、出でて帯方に行きて忽ち宝帯の瑞を得たり。</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collectionsonline.lacma.org/mwebcgi/mweb.exe?request=record;id=104465;type=101|title=Sakanoue no Karitamaro Drawing His Bow|publisher=[[Los Angeles County Museum of Art]]|accessdate=April 2, 2011}}</ref> The Sakanoue clan's {{ill|Family tree|en|Family tree|WD=Q189977|lt=Family tree}} shows that Tamuramaro is a 14th-generation descendant of Emperor Ling.<ref>塙 保己一 [Hanawa hokii] 続群書類従 [Zoku Gunsho Ruiju Series] 従十八巻坂上系図 [Volume 18 Sakagami Clan's family tree]</ref> Other research traces the origins of the Sakanoue clan from the Asian mainland, possibly through [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |title=Yosha Research |access-date=April 4, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120729043128/http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |archive-date=July 29, 2012 }}</ref>
==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior second Rank]] (May 27, 811; posthumous){{cn|date=June 2020}}

date|0811|6|17}}|death_place={{ill|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|1=Heian-kyō}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142|1=Samurai}}|title={{ill|Chamberlain of Japan|WD=Q2948088|1=Chamberlain of Japan}}; {{ill|Chinjufu Takako}}|children={{ill|Kiyono Sakanoue|WD=Q11424802|1=Kiyono Sakanoue}}; {{ill|Q106239190|WD=Q106239190|1=Daughter of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}}; {{ill|Q110454274|WD=Q110454274|1=Sakanoue no Takamichi}}; {{ill|Q110454899|WD=Q110454899|1=Sakanoue no Miiko}}; {{ill|Q11424777|WD=Q11424777|1=Sakanoue no Ōno}}; {{ill|Q11424783|WD=Q11424783|1=Sakanoue no Hiroo}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Haruku|WD=Q11424791|1=Sakanoue no Haruku}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Hirono|WD=Q11424782|1=Sakanoue no Hirono}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Masano|WD=Q11424800|1=Sakanoue no Masano}}|father={{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}|relatives={{ill|Q11424817|WD=Q11424817|1=Sakanoue no Takanushi}}; {{ill|Q45828832|WD=Q45828832|1=Sakanoue no Takayasu}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Matako|WD=Q48764642|1=Sakanoue no Matako}}|family={{ill|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477|1=Sakanoue clan}}|native_name=坂上田村麻呂|resting_place={{ill|Nishinoyama Kofun|WD=Q11629758|1=Nishinoyama Kofun}}}} {{Short description|Japanese general and shogun (758 – 811)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2020}} {{family name hatnote|Sakanoue|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro sw.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai|1=Kikuchi Yōsai}}]] {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Tamuramaro'''|坂上 田村麻呂||758{{spnd}}June 17, 811}} was a {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=court noble|1=Kugyō}}, general and ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' of the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. He served as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Minister of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} and ''Ukon'e no Taisho'' (Major Captain of the Right Division of Inner Palace Guards). He held the ''[[kabane]]'' of [[Sukune|Ōsukune]] and the court Rank of [[Junior Second Rank]] and was awarded the {{ill|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|en|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|WD=Q859418|lt=Order of Second Class|1=Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan}}.
He was the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. He was considered an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}} of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} in legend.<ref>Iwao, Seiichi. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&dq=Sakanoue+no+Karitamaro&pg=PA2329 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon'', p. 2329.]</ref>
==Military career== Serving [[Emperor Kanmu]], Tamuramaro was appointed ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' and given the task of conquering the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} (蝦夷征伐 ''Emishi Seibatsu''), a people native to the north of Honshū, which he subjugated. Recent evidence{{fact|date=February 2022}} suggests that a migration of Emishi from northern Honshū to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] took place sometime between the seventh and eighth centuries, perhaps as a direct result of this policy that pre-dated Tamuramaro's appointment. However, many Emishi remained in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}} as subjects of the expanding {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese Empire and later established independent Fushu domains. After Emperor Kanmu's death, the general continued to serve {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei|1=Emperor Heizei}} and {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} as {{nihongo|Major Counselor|大納言|''dainagon''}} and {{nihongo|[[:en:Hyōbu-shō|Minister of War]]|兵部卿|''Hyōbu-kyō''}}.<ref>Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p. 272.</ref> He was the second person to be given the title of ''Sei-i Taishōgun'' (征夷大将軍). The first to receive this title was {{ill|Ōtomo no Otomaro|en|Ōtomo no Otomaro|WD=Q2089860|lt=Ōtomo no Otomaro|1=Ōtomo no Otomaro}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows]] It is said that the famous {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|WD=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata|1=Tanabata}} festivals and parades of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} (also celebrated in the city of Sendai in Miyagi prefecture), which draw over 3 million people to the prefecture a year, were popularised in remembrance of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's campaign to subdue the tribal societies then living in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]]. These annual ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' are called the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=''Nebuta'' festival in Aomori City|1=Aomori Nebuta Matsuri}} and ''Neputa'' Festival ({{lang|ja|ねぷた祭り}}) in [[:en:Hirosaki City|Hirosaki City]]. They feature a number of gigantic, specially-constructed, illuminated paper floats. These huge festival structures are colourfully painted with mythical figures, and teams of men carry them through the streets as crowds shout encouragement. Aomori's great nebuta lanterns are said to hark back to Tamuramaro's innovative strategy in that early ninth century campaign. According to legend, the taishogun is remembered for having ordered huge illuminated lanterns to be placed at the top of hills; and when the curious Emishi approached these bright lights to investigate, they were captured and subdued.<ref>Boroff, Nicholas. ''National Geographic Traveler Japan'', p. 156.</ref> Until the mid-1990s the prize awarded for the best float of the parades was called the Tamuramaro Prize. However, there is no historical record that Tamuramaro went farther north than {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}}
Tamuramaro's name is linked with payments for construction projects at [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu Temple]] (''Kiyomizu-dera'') in the late 8th century.<ref>Kyoto University of Foreign Studies: [http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/7a154c954fe15a0f49257018002ebff5!OpenDocument Japan 101]</ref>
The {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} tells a story of when {{ill|Fujiwara no Toshihito|en|Fujiwara no Toshihito|WD=Q3090673|lt=Fujiwara no Toshihito|1=Fujiwara no Toshihito}} had fought together with Sakanoue no Tamuramaro to subdue a gang of Emishi bandits that had stood in their way when the duo were passing through the Taya Caves on their way back to Kamakura.
[[File:Kuniyoshi Tsuchiyama.jpg|thumb|''The 53 stations of the Tōkaidō in pairs'' by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] depicting, from the left, the demon of Mount Suzuka, Suzuka Gozen and Sakanoue no Tamuramaro led by her to kill the demon Otakemaru.]]
==Death, memorial and legends== In the year 811 (''[[:en:Kōnin|Kōnin]] 2, 3rd month''), Tamuramaro died at age 54, to the great regret of Emperor Saga, who expressed his sense of loss by distributing large quantities of silk cloth, cotton cloth and rice in honour of his dead counsellor. His bow, arrows, quiver and sword were placed in his coffin by order of the Emperor.<ref name="Titsingh, p. 99">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', p. 99.]</ref>
The Tomb of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro is located in Kanshuji Higashikurisunocho, Yamashina Ward, Kyoto, Japan. He is not the samurai buried at Shōgun-zuka as that was a ceremonial statue of a warrior buried by Emperor Kanmu when he decided to move the capital to Heian-kyo, present day Kyoto.{{cn|date=June 2020}} His descendant {{ill|Urabe no Suetake|en|Urabe no Suetake|WD=Q7899535|lt=Urabe no Suetake|1=Urabe no Suetake}} inherited his bow.
After his death, Tamuramaro became a legendary figure featured in many stories. In these legends, Tamuramaro is not always a warrior who wages war against barbarians, but a hero who exterminates ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}'' and bandits in various areas. According to legend, {{ill|Ootakemaru|en|Ootakemaru|WD=Q30926182|lt=Ootakemaru|1=Ootakemaru}}, one of the Great Three Evil Yokai of Japan, was slain by Tamuramaro. Other stories connect him to {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|WD=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen|1=Suzuka Gozen}}, whom he marries and/or slays.{{cn|date=May 2024}} He had a daughter with her named Shourin. According to the Otogi-zoshi, Shourin is said to have become the guardian deity of the Suzuka Mountains, while in the Okujoruri, it is said she ascended to the level of the Jizō Bodhisattva after her death.
==Ancestry== According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', an official historical record, the Sakanoue clan is descended from {{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=Emperor Ling|1=Emperor Ling of Han}} of the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}.<ref>[[Shoku Nihongi]] 延暦四年六月の条:右衛士督従三位兼下総守坂上大忌寸苅田麿ら表を上りて言さく、「臣らは、本是れ{{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=後漢霊帝|1=Emperor Ling of Han}}の曾孫阿智王の後なり。漢の祚、魏に遷れるとき、阿智王、神牛の教に因りて、出でて帯方に行きて忽ち宝帯の瑞を得たり。</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collectionsonline.lacma.org/mwebcgi/mweb.exe?request=record;id=104465;type=101|title=Sakanoue no Karitamaro Drawing His Bow|publisher=[[Los Angeles County Museum of Art]]|accessdate=April 2, 2011}}</ref> The Sakanoue clan's {{ill|Family tree|en|Family tree|WD=Q189977|lt=Family tree|1=Family tree}} shows that Tamuramaro is a 14th-generation descendant of Emperor Ling.<ref>塙 保己一 [Hanawa hokii] 続群書類従 [Zoku Gunsho Ruiju Series] 従十八巻坂上系図 [Volume 18 Sakagami Clan's family tree]</ref> Other research traces the origins of the Sakanoue clan from the Asian mainland, possibly through [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |title=Yosha Research |access-date=April 4, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120729043128/http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |archive-date=July 29, 2012 }}</ref>

Haruku|WD=Q11424791|1=Sakanoue no Haruku}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Hirono|WD=Q11424782|1=Sakanoue no Hirono}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Masano|WD=Q11424800|1=Sakanoue no Masano}}|father={{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}|relatives={{ill|Q11424817|WD=Q11424817|1=Sakanoue no Takanushi}}; {{ill|Q45828832|WD=Q45828832|1=Sakanoue no Takayasu}}; {{ill|Sakanoue no Matako|WD=Q48764642|1=Sakanoue no Matako}}|family={{ill|Q10928477|WD=Q10928477|1=Sakanoue clan}}|native_name=坂上田村麻呂|resting_place={{ill|Nishinoyama Kofun|WD=Q11629758|1=Nishinoyama Kofun}}}} {{Short description|Japanese general and shogun (758 – 811)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2020}} {{family name hatnote|Sakanoue|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro sw.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai|1=Kikuchi Yōsai}}]] {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Tamuramaro'''|坂上 田村麻呂||758{{spnd}}June 17, 811}} was a {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=court noble|1=Kugyō}}, general and ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' of the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. He served as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Minister of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} and ''Ukon'e no Taisho'' (Major Captain of the Right Division of Inner Palace Guards). He held the ''[[kabane]]'' of [[Sukune|Ōsukune]] and the court Rank of [[Junior Second Rank]] and was awarded the {{ill|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|en|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|WD=Q859418|lt=Order of Second Class|1=Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan}}.
He was the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. He was considered an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}} of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} in legend.<ref>Iwao, Seiichi. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&dq=Sakanoue+no+Karitamaro&pg=PA2329 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon'', p. 2329.]</ref>
==Military career== Serving [[Emperor Kanmu]], Tamuramaro was appointed ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' and given the task of conquering the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} (蝦夷征伐 ''Emishi Seibatsu''), a people native to the north of Honshū, which he subjugated. Recent evidence{{fact|date=February 2022}} suggests that a migration of Emishi from northern Honshū to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] took place sometime between the seventh and eighth centuries, perhaps as a direct result of this policy that pre-dated Tamuramaro's appointment. However, many Emishi remained in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}} as subjects of the expanding {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese Empire and later established independent Fushu domains. After Emperor Kanmu's death, the general continued to serve {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei|1=Emperor Heizei}} and {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} as {{nihongo|Major Counselor|大納言|''dainagon''}} and {{nihongo|[[:en:Hyōbu-shō|Minister of War]]|兵部卿|''Hyōbu-kyō''}}.<ref>Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p. 272.</ref> He was the second person to be given the title of ''Sei-i Taishōgun'' (征夷大将軍). The first to receive this title was {{ill|Ōtomo no Otomaro|en|Ōtomo no Otomaro|WD=Q2089860|lt=Ōtomo no Otomaro|1=Ōtomo no Otomaro}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows]] It is said that the famous {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|WD=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata|1=Tanabata}} festivals and parades of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} (also celebrated in the city of Sendai in Miyagi prefecture), which draw over 3 million people to the prefecture a year, were popularised in remembrance of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's campaign to subdue the tribal societies then living in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]]. These annual ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' are called the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=''Nebuta'' festival in Aomori City|1=Aomori Nebuta Matsuri}} and ''Neputa'' Festival ({{lang|ja|ねぷた祭り}}) in [[:en:Hirosaki City|Hirosaki City]]. They feature a number of gigantic, specially-constructed, illuminated paper floats. These huge festival structures are colourfully painted with mythical figures, and teams of men carry them through the streets as crowds shout encouragement. Aomori's great nebuta lanterns are said to hark back to Tamuramaro's innovative strategy in that early ninth century campaign. According to legend, the taishogun is remembered for having ordered huge illuminated lanterns to be placed at the top of hills; and when the curious Emishi approached these bright lights to investigate, they were captured and subdued.<ref>Boroff, Nicholas. ''National Geographic Traveler Japan'', p. 156.</ref> Until the mid-1990s the prize awarded for the best float of the parades was called the Tamuramaro Prize. However, there is no historical record that Tamuramaro went farther north than {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}}
Tamuramaro's name is linked with payments for construction projects at [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu Temple]] (''Kiyomizu-dera'') in the late 8th century.<ref>Kyoto University of Foreign Studies: [http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/7a154c954fe15a0f49257018002ebff5!OpenDocument Japan 101]</ref>
The {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} tells a story of when {{ill|Fujiwara no Toshihito|en|Fujiwara no Toshihito|WD=Q3090673|lt=Fujiwara no Toshihito|1=Fujiwara no Toshihito}} had fought together with Sakanoue no Tamuramaro to subdue a gang of Emishi bandits that had stood in their way when the duo were passing through the Taya Caves on their way back to Kamakura.
[[File:Kuniyoshi Tsuchiyama.jpg|thumb|''The 53 stations of the Tōkaidō in pairs'' by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] depicting, from the left, the demon of Mount Suzuka, Suzuka Gozen and Sakanoue no Tamuramaro led by her to kill the demon Otakemaru.]]
==Death, memorial and legends== In the year 811 (''[[:en:Kōnin|Kōnin]] 2, 3rd month''), Tamuramaro died at age 54, to the great regret of Emperor Saga, who expressed his sense of loss by distributing large quantities of silk cloth, cotton cloth and rice in honour of his dead counsellor. His bow, arrows, quiver and sword were placed in his coffin by order of the Emperor.<ref name="Titsingh, p. 99">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', p. 99.]</ref>
The Tomb of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro is located in Kanshuji Higashikurisunocho, Yamashina Ward, Kyoto, Japan. He is not the samurai buried at Shōgun-zuka as that was a ceremonial statue of a warrior buried by Emperor Kanmu when he decided to move the capital to Heian-kyo, present day Kyoto.{{cn|date=June 2020}} His descendant {{ill|Urabe no Suetake|en|Urabe no Suetake|WD=Q7899535|lt=Urabe no Suetake|1=Urabe no Suetake}} inherited his bow.
After his death, Tamuramaro became a legendary figure featured in many stories. In these legends, Tamuramaro is not always a warrior who wages war against barbarians, but a hero who exterminates ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}'' and bandits in various areas. According to legend, {{ill|Ootakemaru|en|Ootakemaru|WD=Q30926182|lt=Ootakemaru|1=Ootakemaru}}, one of the Great Three Evil Yokai of Japan, was slain by Tamuramaro. Other stories connect him to {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|WD=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen|1=Suzuka Gozen}}, whom he marries and/or slays.{{cn|date=May 2024}} He had a daughter with her named Shourin. According to the Otogi-zoshi, Shourin is said to have become the guardian deity of the Suzuka Mountains, while in the Okujoruri, it is said she ascended to the level of the Jizō Bodhisattva after her death.
==Ancestry== According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', an official historical record, the Sakanoue clan is descended from {{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=Emperor Ling|1=Emperor Ling of Han}} of the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}.<ref>[[Shoku Nihongi]] 延暦四年六月の条:右衛士督従三位兼下総守坂上大忌寸苅田麿ら表を上りて言さく、「臣らは、本是れ{{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=後漢霊帝|1=Emperor Ling of Han}}の曾孫阿智王の後なり。漢の祚、魏に遷れるとき、阿智王、神牛の教に因りて、出でて帯方に行きて忽ち宝帯の瑞を得たり。</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collectionsonline.lacma.org/mwebcgi/mweb.exe?request=record;id=104465;type=101|title=Sakanoue no Karitamaro Drawing His Bow|publisher=[[Los Angeles County Museum of Art]]|accessdate=April 2, 2011}}</ref> The Sakanoue clan's {{ill|Family tree|en|Family tree|WD=Q189977|lt=Family tree|1=Family tree}} shows that Tamuramaro is a 14th-generation descendant of Emperor Ling.<ref>塙 保己一 [Hanawa hokii] 続群書類従 [Zoku Gunsho Ruiju Series] 従十八巻坂上系図 [Volume 18 Sakagami Clan's family tree]</ref> Other research traces the origins of the Sakanoue clan from the Asian mainland, possibly through [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |title=Yosha Research |access-date=April 4, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120729043128/http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |archive-date=July 29, 2012 }}</ref>

description|Japanese general and shogun (758 – 811)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2020}} {{family name hatnote|Sakanoue|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro sw.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai|1=Kikuchi Yōsai}}]] {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Tamuramaro'''|坂上 田村麻呂||758{{spnd}}June 17, 811}} was a {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=court noble|1=Kugyō}}, general and ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' of the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. He served as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Minister of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} and ''Ukon'e no Taisho'' (Major Captain of the Right Division of Inner Palace Guards). He held the ''[[kabane]]'' of [[Sukune|Ōsukune]] and the court Rank of [[Junior Second Rank]] and was awarded the {{ill|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|en|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|WD=Q859418|lt=Order of Second Class|1=Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan}}.
He was the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. He was considered an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}} of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} in legend.<ref>Iwao, Seiichi. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&dq=Sakanoue+no+Karitamaro&pg=PA2329 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon'', p. 2329.]</ref>
==Military career== Serving [[Emperor Kanmu]], Tamuramaro was appointed ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' and given the task of conquering the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} (蝦夷征伐 ''Emishi Seibatsu''), a people native to the north of Honshū, which he subjugated. Recent evidence{{fact|date=February 2022}} suggests that a migration of Emishi from northern Honshū to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] took place sometime between the seventh and eighth centuries, perhaps as a direct result of this policy that pre-dated Tamuramaro's appointment. However, many Emishi remained in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}} as subjects of the expanding {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese Empire and later established independent Fushu domains. After Emperor Kanmu's death, the general continued to serve {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei|1=Emperor Heizei}} and {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} as {{nihongo|Major Counselor|大納言|''dainagon''}} and {{nihongo|[[:en:Hyōbu-shō|Minister of War]]|兵部卿|''Hyōbu-kyō''}}.<ref>Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p. 272.</ref> He was the second person to be given the title of ''Sei-i Taishōgun'' (征夷大将軍). The first to receive this title was {{ill|Ōtomo no Otomaro|en|Ōtomo no Otomaro|WD=Q2089860|lt=Ōtomo no Otomaro|1=Ōtomo no Otomaro}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows]] It is said that the famous {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|WD=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata|1=Tanabata}} festivals and parades of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} (also celebrated in the city of Sendai in Miyagi prefecture), which draw over 3 million people to the prefecture a year, were popularised in remembrance of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's campaign to subdue the tribal societies then living in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]]. These annual ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' are called the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=''Nebuta'' festival in Aomori City|1=Aomori Nebuta Matsuri}} and ''Neputa'' Festival ({{lang|ja|ねぷた祭り}}) in [[:en:Hirosaki City|Hirosaki City]]. They feature a number of gigantic, specially-constructed, illuminated paper floats. These huge festival structures are colourfully painted with mythical figures, and teams of men carry them through the streets as crowds shout encouragement. Aomori's great nebuta lanterns are said to hark back to Tamuramaro's innovative strategy in that early ninth century campaign. According to legend, the taishogun is remembered for having ordered huge illuminated lanterns to be placed at the top of hills; and when the curious Emishi approached these bright lights to investigate, they were captured and subdued.<ref>Boroff, Nicholas. ''National Geographic Traveler Japan'', p. 156.</ref> Until the mid-1990s the prize awarded for the best float of the parades was called the Tamuramaro Prize. However, there is no historical record that Tamuramaro went farther north than {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}}
Tamuramaro's name is linked with payments for construction projects at [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu Temple]] (''Kiyomizu-dera'') in the late 8th century.<ref>Kyoto University of Foreign Studies: [http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/7a154c954fe15a0f49257018002ebff5!OpenDocument Japan 101]</ref>
The {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} tells a story of when {{ill|Fujiwara no Toshihito|en|Fujiwara no Toshihito|WD=Q3090673|lt=Fujiwara no Toshihito|1=Fujiwara no Toshihito}} had fought together with Sakanoue no Tamuramaro to subdue a gang of Emishi bandits that had stood in their way when the duo were passing through the Taya Caves on their way back to Kamakura.
[[File:Kuniyoshi Tsuchiyama.jpg|thumb|''The 53 stations of the Tōkaidō in pairs'' by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] depicting, from the left, the demon of Mount Suzuka, Suzuka Gozen and Sakanoue no Tamuramaro led by her to kill the demon Otakemaru.]]
==Death, memorial and legends== In the year 811 (''[[:en:Kōnin|Kōnin]] 2, 3rd month''), Tamuramaro died at age 54, to the great regret of Emperor Saga, who expressed his sense of loss by distributing large quantities of silk cloth, cotton cloth and rice in honour of his dead counsellor. His bow, arrows, quiver and sword were placed in his coffin by order of the Emperor.<ref name="Titsingh, p. 99">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', p. 99.]</ref>
The Tomb of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro is located in Kanshuji Higashikurisunocho, Yamashina Ward, Kyoto, Japan. He is not the samurai buried at Shōgun-zuka as that was a ceremonial statue of a warrior buried by Emperor Kanmu when he decided to move the capital to Heian-kyo, present day Kyoto.{{cn|date=June 2020}} His descendant {{ill|Urabe no Suetake|en|Urabe no Suetake|WD=Q7899535|lt=Urabe no Suetake|1=Urabe no Suetake}} inherited his bow.
After his death, Tamuramaro became a legendary figure featured in many stories. In these legends, Tamuramaro is not always a warrior who wages war against barbarians, but a hero who exterminates ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}'' and bandits in various areas. According to legend, {{ill|Ootakemaru|en|Ootakemaru|WD=Q30926182|lt=Ootakemaru|1=Ootakemaru}}, one of the Great Three Evil Yokai of Japan, was slain by Tamuramaro. Other stories connect him to {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|WD=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen|1=Suzuka Gozen}}, whom he marries and/or slays.{{cn|date=May 2024}} He had a daughter with her named Shourin. According to the Otogi-zoshi, Shourin is said to have become the guardian deity of the Suzuka Mountains, while in the Okujoruri, it is said she ascended to the level of the Jizō Bodhisattva after her death.
==Ancestry== According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', an official historical record, the Sakanoue clan is descended from {{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=Emperor Ling|1=Emperor Ling of Han}} of the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}.<ref>[[Shoku Nihongi]] 延暦四年六月の条:右衛士督従三位兼下総守坂上大忌寸苅田麿ら表を上りて言さく、「臣らは、本是れ{{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=後漢霊帝|1=Emperor Ling of Han}}の曾孫阿智王の後なり。漢の祚、魏に遷れるとき、阿智王、神牛の教に因りて、出でて帯方に行きて忽ち宝帯の瑞を得たり。</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collectionsonline.lacma.org/mwebcgi/mweb.exe?request=record;id=104465;type=101|title=Sakanoue no Karitamaro Drawing His Bow|publisher=[[Los Angeles County Museum of Art]]|accessdate=April 2, 2011}}</ref> The Sakanoue clan's {{ill|Family tree|en|Family tree|WD=Q189977|lt=Family tree|1=Family tree}} shows that Tamuramaro is a 14th-generation descendant of Emperor Ling.<ref>塙 保己一 [Hanawa hokii] 続群書類従 [Zoku Gunsho Ruiju Series] 従十八巻坂上系図 [Volume 18 Sakagami Clan's family tree]</ref> Other research traces the origins of the Sakanoue clan from the Asian mainland, possibly through [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |title=Yosha Research |access-date=April 4, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120729043128/http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |archive-date=July 29, 2012 }}</ref>
==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior second Rank]] (May 27, 811; posthumous){{cn|date=June 2020}}

description|Japanese general and shogun (758 – 811)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2020}} {{family name hatnote|Sakanoue|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro sw.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro by {{ill|Kikuchi Yōsai|en|Kikuchi Yōsai|WD=Q700019|lt=Kikuchi Yōsai|1=Kikuchi Yōsai}}]] {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Tamuramaro'''|坂上 田村麻呂||758{{spnd}}June 17, 811}} was a {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=court noble|1=Kugyō}}, general and ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' of the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. He served as {{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}, {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Minister of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} and ''Ukon'e no Taisho'' (Major Captain of the Right Division of Inner Palace Guards). He held the ''[[kabane]]'' of [[Sukune|Ōsukune]] and the court Rank of [[Junior Second Rank]] and was awarded the {{ill|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|en|Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan|WD=Q859418|lt=Order of Second Class|1=Orders, decorations, and medals of Japan}}.
He was the son of {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|WD=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. He was considered an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}} of {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} in legend.<ref>Iwao, Seiichi. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&dq=Sakanoue+no+Karitamaro&pg=PA2329 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon'', p. 2329.]</ref>
==Military career== Serving [[Emperor Kanmu]], Tamuramaro was appointed ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' and given the task of conquering the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} (蝦夷征伐 ''Emishi Seibatsu''), a people native to the north of Honshū, which he subjugated. Recent evidence{{fact|date=February 2022}} suggests that a migration of Emishi from northern Honshū to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] took place sometime between the seventh and eighth centuries, perhaps as a direct result of this policy that pre-dated Tamuramaro's appointment. However, many Emishi remained in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}} as subjects of the expanding {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese Empire and later established independent Fushu domains. After Emperor Kanmu's death, the general continued to serve {{ill|Emperor Heizei|en|Emperor Heizei|WD=Q353558|lt=Emperor Heizei|1=Emperor Heizei}} and {{ill|Emperor Saga|en|Emperor Saga|WD=Q316654|lt=Emperor Saga|1=Emperor Saga}} as {{nihongo|Major Counselor|大納言|''dainagon''}} and {{nihongo|[[:en:Hyōbu-shō|Minister of War]]|兵部卿|''Hyōbu-kyō''}}.<ref>Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' p. 272.</ref> He was the second person to be given the title of ''Sei-i Taishōgun'' (征夷大将軍). The first to receive this title was {{ill|Ōtomo no Otomaro|en|Ōtomo no Otomaro|WD=Q2089860|lt=Ōtomo no Otomaro|1=Ōtomo no Otomaro}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}} [[File:Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows.jpg|thumb|Sakanoue Tamuramaro in a rain of arrows]] It is said that the famous {{ill|Tanabata|en|Tanabata|WD=Q1193996|lt=Tanabata|1=Tanabata}} festivals and parades of {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}} (also celebrated in the city of Sendai in Miyagi prefecture), which draw over 3 million people to the prefecture a year, were popularised in remembrance of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro's campaign to subdue the tribal societies then living in [[:en:Tōhoku|Tōhoku]]. These annual ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' are called the {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=''Nebuta'' festival in Aomori City|1=Aomori Nebuta Matsuri}} and ''Neputa'' Festival ({{lang|ja|ねぷた祭り}}) in [[:en:Hirosaki City|Hirosaki City]]. They feature a number of gigantic, specially-constructed, illuminated paper floats. These huge festival structures are colourfully painted with mythical figures, and teams of men carry them through the streets as crowds shout encouragement. Aomori's great nebuta lanterns are said to hark back to Tamuramaro's innovative strategy in that early ninth century campaign. According to legend, the taishogun is remembered for having ordered huge illuminated lanterns to be placed at the top of hills; and when the curious Emishi approached these bright lights to investigate, they were captured and subdued.<ref>Boroff, Nicholas. ''National Geographic Traveler Japan'', p. 156.</ref> Until the mid-1990s the prize awarded for the best float of the parades was called the Tamuramaro Prize. However, there is no historical record that Tamuramaro went farther north than {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}.{{cn|date=June 2020}}
Tamuramaro's name is linked with payments for construction projects at [[Kiyomizu-dera|Kiyomizu Temple]] (''Kiyomizu-dera'') in the late 8th century.<ref>Kyoto University of Foreign Studies: [http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/7a154c954fe15a0f49257018002ebff5!OpenDocument Japan 101]</ref>
The {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} tells a story of when {{ill|Fujiwara no Toshihito|en|Fujiwara no Toshihito|WD=Q3090673|lt=Fujiwara no Toshihito|1=Fujiwara no Toshihito}} had fought together with Sakanoue no Tamuramaro to subdue a gang of Emishi bandits that had stood in their way when the duo were passing through the Taya Caves on their way back to Kamakura.
[[File:Kuniyoshi Tsuchiyama.jpg|thumb|''The 53 stations of the Tōkaidō in pairs'' by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] depicting, from the left, the demon of Mount Suzuka, Suzuka Gozen and Sakanoue no Tamuramaro led by her to kill the demon Otakemaru.]]
==Death, memorial and legends== In the year 811 (''[[:en:Kōnin|Kōnin]] 2, 3rd month''), Tamuramaro died at age 54, to the great regret of Emperor Saga, who expressed his sense of loss by distributing large quantities of silk cloth, cotton cloth and rice in honour of his dead counsellor. His bow, arrows, quiver and sword were placed in his coffin by order of the Emperor.<ref name="Titsingh, p. 99">Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', p. 99.]</ref>
The Tomb of Sakanoue no Tamuramaro is located in Kanshuji Higashikurisunocho, Yamashina Ward, Kyoto, Japan. He is not the samurai buried at Shōgun-zuka as that was a ceremonial statue of a warrior buried by Emperor Kanmu when he decided to move the capital to Heian-kyo, present day Kyoto.{{cn|date=June 2020}} His descendant {{ill|Urabe no Suetake|en|Urabe no Suetake|WD=Q7899535|lt=Urabe no Suetake|1=Urabe no Suetake}} inherited his bow.
After his death, Tamuramaro became a legendary figure featured in many stories. In these legends, Tamuramaro is not always a warrior who wages war against barbarians, but a hero who exterminates ''{{ill|Oni|en|Oni|WD=Q730052|lt=Oni|1=Oni}}'' and bandits in various areas. According to legend, {{ill|Ootakemaru|en|Ootakemaru|WD=Q30926182|lt=Ootakemaru|1=Ootakemaru}}, one of the Great Three Evil Yokai of Japan, was slain by Tamuramaro. Other stories connect him to {{ill|Suzuka Gozen|en|Suzuka Gozen|WD=Q11649495|lt=Suzuka Gozen|1=Suzuka Gozen}}, whom he marries and/or slays.{{cn|date=May 2024}} He had a daughter with her named Shourin. According to the Otogi-zoshi, Shourin is said to have become the guardian deity of the Suzuka Mountains, while in the Okujoruri, it is said she ascended to the level of the Jizō Bodhisattva after her death.
==Ancestry== According to the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'', an official historical record, the Sakanoue clan is descended from {{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=Emperor Ling|1=Emperor Ling of Han}} of the Chinese {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}}.<ref>[[Shoku Nihongi]] 延暦四年六月の条:右衛士督従三位兼下総守坂上大忌寸苅田麿ら表を上りて言さく、「臣らは、本是れ{{ill|Emperor Ling of Han|en|Emperor Ling of Han|WD=Q7299|lt=後漢霊帝|1=Emperor Ling of Han}}の曾孫阿智王の後なり。漢の祚、魏に遷れるとき、阿智王、神牛の教に因りて、出でて帯方に行きて忽ち宝帯の瑞を得たり。</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://collectionsonline.lacma.org/mwebcgi/mweb.exe?request=record;id=104465;type=101|title=Sakanoue no Karitamaro Drawing His Bow|publisher=[[Los Angeles County Museum of Art]]|accessdate=April 2, 2011}}</ref> The Sakanoue clan's {{ill|Family tree|en|Family tree|WD=Q189977|lt=Family tree|1=Family tree}} shows that Tamuramaro is a 14th-generation descendant of Emperor Ling.<ref>塙 保己一 [Hanawa hokii] 続群書類従 [Zoku Gunsho Ruiju Series] 従十八巻坂上系図 [Volume 18 Sakagami Clan's family tree]</ref> Other research traces the origins of the Sakanoue clan from the Asian mainland, possibly through [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |title=Yosha Research |access-date=April 4, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120729043128/http://members.jcom.home.ne.jp/yosha/yr/nationalism/Reports_from_early_records.html |archive-date=July 29, 2012 }}</ref>
